data.title: Federal Reserve Holds Rates Steady, But Could FOMC Lose Control?
data.description:
As widely expected, the Federal Reserve has opted to hold the Federal Funds Rate steady. So far, markets haven’t shown any major reaction to the news, but I suspect we’ll see condemnation from President Donald Trump, who would prefer rate cuts in an effort to stimulate the economy. Increasingly, however, I wonder if the Federal Reserve could start to lose influence over interest rates owing to the rising risks of fiscal dominance, the declining value of the dollar, and increasing concerns over the United States’ financial health.

Before jumping into my concerns, I’ll recap the outcomes of the FOMC meeting to provide some context. I assumed the Federal Reserve would hold rates steady as soon as the Trump Administration moved forward with an investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell. If nothing else, Powell will want to reassert the Fed’s independence, which has already come into question. Further, whoever Trump appoints as the Federal Reserve Chair will likely push for more rate cuts and potentially steeper rate cuts. Powell may make moves now to keep interest rates higher than he otherwise would in an effort to soften future cuts once he vacates the chair position. (I’m assuming that the FOMC will follow tradition and thus the Chair’s lead on cut recommendations. We could also see a Committee revolt, with members opposing the Chair’s recommendations.)

Powell noted that the United States economy expanded at a “solid pace” in 2025. Job growth appears to have remained low (especially compared to how strong the economy otherwise performed), but the unemployment rate is stabilizing after a period of softening. Powell cited a decline in migration and labor force participation as contributing to slowing labor force growth. Openings, layoffs, and hirings over the last three months have, by and large, shown little change. Inflation, meanwhile, has remained elevated. While inflation has not increased as much as many feared owing to tariffs, American consumers are under pressure, and any accelerating increases in inflation could prove especially detrimental.

For now, the Federal Reserve looks set to hold pat for now and to continue to digest data as it rolls in. Unless surprising data starts to pop up, such as a deterioration of the labor market or rapidly accelerating inflation, I expect the Federal Reserve will continue to maintain current rates. Yet at the same time, I worry that the Federal Reserve may begin to lose some of the influence it holds over interest rates and especially short-term Treasury yields. Three-year yields have remained stubbornly high despite rate cuts, and the declining value of the dollar could result in investors wanting more compensation, as could the shaky financial health of the U.S. government.

I believe there is a risk that interest rates could begin to rise even in spite of actions by the Federal Reserve. If so, this could reduce the ability of policymakers to control and stimulate the economy. As interest rates rise, it could threaten to slow the economy as borrowing costs increase. Declining faith in the American government, investment markets, and even the basic framework of the global economy could further spook investors and weigh on markets. While I believe the risks of that unfolding any time soon are low, investors should at least consider the risks, watch for red flags, and plan defensive strategies. Planning doesn’t mean you have to act, but it puts you in a better position should the time come when you need to act.

Concerns Regarding The FOMC’s Ability To Influence Interest Rates

The FOMC has a major impact on interest rates, and especially short-term interest rates. Raising the Federal Funds rate makes borrowing in general more expensive, and lowering the rate makes it cheaper. Yet interest rates for Treasuries and most other things are still set in markets. FOMC rate changes can have an impact on that market, but at the end of the day, the Federal Funds Rate is only one of many factors. I believe there is a serious risk that some factors could start to have a more pronounced impact on interest rates, specifically for Treasuries, but keep in mind that Treasury interest rates impact other rates and the broader economy.

First, there’s the risk of fiscal dominance, with the need to keep interest rate payments on the federal debt. In this case, we could see the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate interrupted, with a third concern muscling its way in and potentially to the forefront of the Reserve’s concerns. Remember, the Federal Reserve is charged with moderating inflation and encouraging full employment. This is typically done by adjusting the overnight interest rates. Increasing rates should cool the economy and thus moderate inflation. Cutting rates encourages borrowing and stimulates the economy. With fiscal dominance, the Federal Reserve may be encouraged to keep rates low in an effort to keep debt payments affordable for the Federal government. This means rates aren’t responding to inflation or employment but instead to another factor. As a result, the Federal Funds rate might be kept low even in the face of elevated inflation, and investors in turn would demand higher interest rate payments even on short-term Treasures.

Federal debt has continued to grow both nominally and as a percent of the GDP. While the Trump Administration is aiming for GDP growth to exceed debt growth, actually achieving that will be far easier said than done. If the GDP can outgrow debt, it would decrease but not fully resolve the pressure. Yet after a brief decline in Q2 2025, debt to GDP rose in Q3. I wouldn’t be surprised if debt drops a bit once the Q4 numbers are computed, but I’d want to see declines over several quarters before putting much faith behind long-term reductions.

Additionally, and I think more importantly, the financial health of the United States may increasingly come into question. This could be fueled by concerns over fiscal dominance alone. Once the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate is upended in this scenario, it could spook investors. Many other issues could also rattle investors and shake confidence in America’s financial health and its ability to repay its debts. Debt interest payments hit $970 billion in 2025 and are expected to top $1 trillion in 2026. Through the first 3 months in FY26, interest rate payment costs were up more than 10% compared to prior years.

If the United States government doesn’t get a handle on its financial situation, investors may start to demand more compensation to hold debt. Long-term debt is probably the most at risk, and we have seen rates for 30-year Treasuries and 10-year Treasuries tick up over the last few months, despite rate cuts. (To be clear, FOMC cuts are more closely correlated with short-term bonds, not long-term.)

Yields for bonds for 2 years or less are continuing to track with FOMC rate cuts. Interestingly, yields with 3-year bonds may be decoupling a bit. Three-year bond rates don’t track FOMC cuts as closely as shorter-term bonds but typically do track, as shown in the first chart below. Yet over the last few months, 3-year yields have held steady and ticked up slightly despite rate cuts, as shown in the second chart. This doesn’t prove any sort of long-term decoupling, but it's something I plan to keep an eye on going forward.

On top of the above, investors may demand higher interest rates if the dollar continues to slide. We’re seeing some evidence of this occurring already. Comments by President Trump earlier this week, noting that he wasn’t concerned about the dollar’s slide, had a notable effect on bond yields, as noted by Bloomberg. The dollar has been sliding against various currencies over the last year. Especially international investors and large institutions may start to take that into account more often.

Add in general geopolitical instability, concerns over an AI bubble sinking America’s economy, and various other issues, and I believe there’s a genuine risk that interest rates will become less predictable and influenceable by FOMC decisions going forward. We have seen similar scenarios in emerging markets in recent memory, but traditionally, this has been borderline unfathomable with the United States. While I don’t believe there is any reason to panic, I do think investors should consider the possibility of the Federal Reserve losing some ability to influence interest rates and the economy. Further, severe sovereign financial duress shouldn’t be ruled out.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The United States economy and the American dollar remain central to the global economy and global financial markets. They are bedrocks and won’t be easily shaken or disturbed. There are currently no real alternatives to the dollar, and U.S. Treasuries play crucial roles in the global financial system. Additionally, if the American economy continues to grow at a rapid rate, it could outgrow the current headwinds it is facing.

Key Takeaway: Investors Should Keep The U.S. Government's Financial Health In Mind

The financial stability of the American government has long been taken for granted by many. While I don’t believe the government’s financial health is at serious risk right now, I do believe more questions and concerns are popping up, and investors should keep an eye on things. If the Federal Reserve starts to lose its ability to manage the economy, it could cause serious economic challenges and result in severe market turbulence.

data.title: IonQ: More Cash Down The Drain
data.description:
The quantum computing space is still generally pre-revenue, but IonQ, Inc. (IONQ) continues down a path of acquiring companies with existing revenue streams giving the stock market the appearance of a booming revenue stream. The company just bought a semiconductor fab for vertical integration while the AI computing world is highly focused on specialization. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on IonQ due to the complexity of managing businesses in a vertical integration when the chip market just went hyper-specialized.

Low Value For A Reason

IonQ has agreed to acquire SkyWater Technology (SKYT) for $35 per share, or $1.8 billion. The company is a leading U.S. based, pure-play semiconductor foundry focusing on foundational nodes and advanced packaging.

The deal includes IonQ paying $15 per share in cash and $20 in stock with a collar. The collar restricts the upside up at $60.13 with a payout to SkyWater shareholders at 0.3326 IonQ shares, but the real focus is the lower limit at $37.99 (trades at $46 now) with the SkyWater shareholder receiving 0.5265 IonQ shares.

SkyWater had recently forecast a 2026 revenue target of at least $600 million, so the valuation is a relatively low at just 3x forward sales. The company had just reported Q3 '25 revenues of $151 million suggesting SkyWater isn't forecasting any sequential growth in 2026 after acquiring the Texas fab from Infineon Technologies (IFNNY) in June.

The big question is why SkyWater wanted to cash out nearly 40% of the deal via cash at such a limited valuation when the semiconductor foundry market is in high demand due to AI computing demands. Either IonQ got an incredible steal or SkyWater doesn't have the real growth prospects to warrant holding out for a much higher valuation.

IonQ has now created a vertically integrated system from chip design to quantum computer production to chip manufacturing. The company is much more like the Intel (INTC) vertical model that worked when the company was a leading foundry, but Intel was unable to keep up with TSMC (TSM) on manufacturing the best chips from the best chip designers in a hyper focused world.

The quantum computing company claims the acquisition enables IonQ to accelerate quantum computing roadmap and secure the supply chain to make a computer with 200,000 physical qubits and 8,000 logical qubits a year sooner The big question is why SkyWater would partially cash out at only $1.8 billion, if the demand was so great that IonQ needs to buy the foundry business to secure supply for this 2028 ramp.

IBM (IBM) is only forecasting reaching 1,080 physical qubits by 2028. Not only is IonQ forecasting this huge ramp to surpass the goals of a large quantum company like IBM, but IonQ is suggesting the ability to pull forward the path to 200,000 physical qubits by a year in what appears a very improbably path.

SkyWater Tech. already added 4 new quantum customers since the end of Q2 and announced quantum computing revenue was up 30% in 2025. These customers will not be excited to fund competition.

Pure Play No Longer

Along with the merger announcement, IonQ reported 2025 revenues likely reached $110 million. The company ended Q3 with a $3.5 billion cash on the balance sheet and this transaction will take $770 million in cash.

The quantum computing company was forecast to reach 2026 sales of $192 million. IonQ is suddenly a semiconducting foundry business, not a quantum computing business as SkyWater is forecasting 2026 revenues of $600 million.

The new 2026 revenue forecast sits at $800 million with 75% of sales from the foundry business. IonQ faces the real risk of these new quantum customers looking elsewhere for a foundry partner considering IonQ is a competitor, similar to how Intel struggles to get foundry business from Apple (AAPL), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Nvidia (NVDA) due to competing on chip design and the concerns on IP protections.

The stock has a current market cap of $16 billion and the deal will add ~$1 billion in additional shares. The kicker here is that IonQ will report limited pro-forma growth with $600 million of revenue with no growth and a good portion of the IonQ business to end 2025 from acquisitions, not organic growth.

IonQ trades at 84x the 2026 sales target. What this deal does is change this valuation equation with the stock suddenly trading closer to 21x forward sales due to buying a foundry business at only 3x sales targets, but the pro-forma growth rate will dip to only in the 10% range.

If IonQ is able to hit these very aggressive qubit targets due to vertical integration, the stock could have considerable upside. In addition, the market might interpret the sudden jump in 2026 revenues as IonQ leading the quantum computing space, injecting some excitement in the stock price.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that IonQ is now a far-flung business, which are very hard to manage. The quantum computing company has already announced another deal to buy Seed Innovations for AI-optimized quantum performance software before the ink is hardly dry and the focus should be on integrating the new fab business.

Investors should be very careful if the current support around $40+ doesn't hold. Otherwise, the market excitement around the quantum business could send IonQ on another quantum filled run higher despite the company just burning cash and diluting shareholders at this point.

data.title: Debt, Debt, Debt...
data.description:
"Record or near-record debt in the United States, Britain, France, Italy, and Japan threatens to hamstring growth and sow financial instability around the globe," writes Patricia Cohen in the New York Times.

The United States is contributing its share to the narrative.

"The U.S. national debt is now $38 trillion, roughly 125 percent the size of the American economy."

In the United States, "Net interest payments have tripled over the past five years, reaching roughly $1 trillion."

"They now eat up 15 percent of U.S. spending, the second biggest expense after social security."

William G. Gale, Arjay and Frances Miller Chair in Federal Economic Policy and also co-director of the Tax Policy Center at the Brookings Institution and the author of "Fiscal Therapy: Curing America's Debt Addiction and Investing in the Future" (Oxford University Press, 2019), has warned "that the continuing prospect of growing debt threatens the country's role as an economic leader and undermines investor confidence in Treasury bonds and the dollar."

Ms. Cohen warns:"For decades, crushing debt has spread misery in the world's poor and lower-income nations. But the menace of unsupportable borrowing that now hangs over the global economy emanates from some of the richest countries."

Debt is good. But too much debt is...well...not so good.

I have been in some kind of "finance" all of my professional life.

I have managed three banks.

Jokingly, but with a wry smile on my face, I have argued throughout my career that the philosophy behind my "banking practice" is that the only people that you should make loans to are people who don't need the money.

Loans are a good thing. But too many loans are...well...not so good.

Furthermore, for most of my career, government borrowing has been looked on with a very favorable eye.

For most of my career, government borrowing to help manage an economy has been quite attractive.

"Keynesian" economics has dominated the discussion in this field.

But a "good thing" can get out of control.

A "good thing" can turn into a very bad thing if not managed well.

A little debt can work out just fine.

A little more debt can be added to the good work.

And a little more debt can seemingly never hurt.

Then, some other "good" opportunities arise, and a little more debt appears worth the commitment.

And so on, and so on.

Ms. Cohen adds...

"And perhaps most worrisome, overhanging debt--pumped up even when an economy is relatively sound and jobless rates are low, like in the United States--gives governments less room to respond when things sour."

Cohen quotes a well-known Harvard economics professor: "You want to be able to spend big and spend fast when you need to."

The question here is who determines when it is time "when you need to"...or "when you don't need to."

Once you get to borrowing...and borrowing...and borrowing...it is very, very, very, very, very difficult to stop.

"Oh," the budget builders say, "we will be disciplined. We will know when it is time to STOP borrowing."

The problem is that politicians have difficulty building up 'this' discipline.

Politicians need to get re-elected.

A little more spending here...a little more spending there.

Maybe a little tax cut....

As Ms. Cohen writes,

"Record or near-record debt...threatens to hamstring growth and sow financial instability around the globe."

This "threatens to hamstring growth and sow financial instability around the globe."

Unfortunately, dealing with the debt still gets pushed out further into the future.

So, what about this "Keynesian" economics?

"Keynesian" economics is not being used for managing the economy...any economy...over the business cycle.

"Keynesian" economics is primarily used as a "tool" for the politicians to achieve their political goals. And, given the way it is now used, "Keynesian" economics is pushed to an extreme...and then pushed further...and then pushed further...with each political party hoping that the "collapse" of the financial system will not come at a time when they are in control of the government.

Debt...according to Ms. Cohen..."threatens to hamstring growth and sow financial instability around the globe."

And the stockpiling of debt destroys trust.

I have just written about trust and the important role that trust plays in the functioning of financial markets.

If you don't have trust built up in your financial markets...well...then you don't really know what might happen.

And investors start moving their money all over the place.

Well, what really stands out today is that the price of gold seems to be "breaking through the ceiling."

Just today, the price of gold is up by $240.00 or more.

The slope of the curve is becoming steeper.

Watch out.

Some forecasts are for gold to reach $600.00 or even higher in the near future.

This is a time of volatility.

This is a time to "watch out"!!!

The Fed's Federal Open Market Committee met today.

The Fed kept its policy rate of interest where it was. No decreases in sight.

The value of the U.S. dollar in foreign exchange markets is up very modestly.

The stock market...well, the Dow Jones Industrial Average and the S&P 500 are down slightly.

The NASDAQ is up slightly.

Most financial markets seem to be relatively calm...for the time being.

Investors...stay alert!!!

The battle continues!!!

Something has to be done about the worldwide excessive use of debt.

data.title: Strong Asset Quality Still Supports A Buy Rating For First Community Corporation
data.description:
Earlier this morning, First Community Corporation (FCCO) released its final quarterly results for 2025. GAAP earnings of $0.66 per share missed expectations by $0.02 and fell short of earnings in Q3 by $0.05. For the full year, the picture for the company looked better with diluted earnings per share of $2.47, 36.5% ahead of the $1.81 recorded for 2024.

The company's results for the past six months have been impacted by its acquisition of Signature Bank of Georgia that was completed three weeks ago. Without merger-related costs, FCCO would have earned $0.69 per share in Q4. Earnings growth played a major role in my previous assessment of the company when I wrote about it in August of 2025. I called FCCO a Buy at that time, and since that August analysis was published, FCCO has posted a total return of 9.8%, just ahead of the 9.3% gain from the S&P 500 (SP500).

The company has a forward P/E in the single digits and an attractive banking footprint in South Carolina and Georgia. I still believe that 2026 is shaping up to be a good year to be an FCCO shareholder, but I do have some concerns about the company’s ability to continue to grow profits at the pace that it has over the past year. I am maintaining my Buy rating for now, as the bank has been able to maintain a high-quality loan portfolio in 2025. However, I have just a little less enthusiasm for the future for reasons discussed below.

Company Overview and Balance Sheet Review

First Community Corporation is the holding company for First Community Bank. The company operates 23 total branches with 20 in its home state of South Carolina. There are two additional First Community bank locations in Augusta, GA, and the new addition from the Signature Bank acquisition in Sandy Springs, just outside of Atlanta. A map of the First Community Bank locations with its latest addition just outside of Atlanta is shown below.

First Community Corporation currently has a market cap of $222.2 million, making it a microcap company by most definitions. (First Community Corporation should not be confused with First Community Bankshares, a community bank based in Virginia valued at $624 million with the ticker symbol of FCBC.) Small banks like FCCO pose greater risks to investors as their earnings can be significantly impacted by the deterioration of a small number of loans.

As of December 31, First Community Corporation had total assets worth $2.06 billion, an increase of 5.1% from the same time in the previous year. Those assets included a portfolio of investment securities worth $492 million and loans valued at $1.32 billion. FCCO was able to grow its loan portfolio by 7.4% in 2025.

If it follows its same reporting schedule as last year, First Community will not file its annual report with the SEC until March. A detailed breakdown of the bank’s loan mix was not provided in its financial update from today, but we can get a good idea of its composition from FCCO’s Q3 10-Q shown below. Commercial real estate is the largest loan allocation by far, representing 65.4% of the Q3 loan total. This percentage has been relatively consistent over the last year, just slightly higher than the 65.3% weight given at the end of 2024. Construction loans are a distant second in the mix at 11.9%. Residential mortgages also have a double-digit share of the outstanding loans at 10.3%.

At the end of 2025, FCCO’s total deposits were $1.75, an increase of 4.4% from the end of the previous year. Total deposit growth was 5.0% excluding brokered deposits which the bank eliminated in 2025. Non-interest-bearing deposits decreased by $16 million when compared to the end of 2024, but they still represented a high level of the bank’s total at 26.7%. The bank’s total cost of deposits was just 1.73% in Q4 2025.

First Community Corporation pays a quarterly dividend of $0.16 and has raised its annual payout for four straight years. In 2024 and 2025, the increase was not announced until the third quarter of the year. FCCO has a forward yield of 2.21% at its current rate.

Analysis of First Community Corporation

There is a lot to like about last year’s results at First Community Corporation that go beyond just the growth in profits, but the improvements on the company’s bottom line were substantial. On a year-over-year basis, FCCO grew GAAP profits by over 36%. Without the costs associated with the Signature Bank deal, profits would have grown over 44%. The analysts that cover the company project it will earn $2.91 in FY 2026 and $3.13 in FY 2027.

Under the surface of those headline numbers are operational metrics that have been improving for an extended period of time. Net interest margin for the bank was 3.32% in Q4, which marked the seventh straight quarter of margin improvement. Over the last 12 months, return on assets at the bank has risen 20 basis points from 0.74% to 0.94%. Return on equity has ballooned from 10.17% to 12.36%. The bank has also lowered its efficiency ratio from 71.56% to 65.97%.

Additional data available on the company’s income statement supports an optimistic view of FCCO’s future. Over the last 12 months, net interest income rose 19.2% to $62.0 million. This was caused by both an increase in interest income and a decrease in interest expense. Non-interest income improved 21.0% to $16.9 million in FY 2025. The main drivers behind this were a 38.1% increase in mortgage banking income and a 22.4% enhancement in the company’s fees and commissions from its wealth management division. The company’s assets under management totaled a record $1.170 billion as of December 31, 2025. At the end of 2024, the number was under $1 billion at just $926.0 million.

Perhaps the most impressive results from the company could be seen in its asset quality metrics which were able to remain strong in the face of some notable headwinds for commercial real estate. At the end of 2024, net charge offs for the bank stood at 0.01%. Somehow, FCCO was able to improve on that number, as it stood at 0.00% at the end of 2025. Non-performing assets stood at just 0.02% at the end of last year. Loans past due represented just 0.07% of the total loans outstanding.

When the bank’s Q1 2026 report is issued, it should get a noticeable boost from the addition of Signature Bank. The Sandy Springs branch had a strong Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio of almost 16% and leverage ratio of 13.87%, both significantly higher than what FCCO has now. Signature’s net interest margin of 4.63% is over 100 basis points ahead of First Community Bank. The deal is expected to be accretive to First Community's earnings per share by about 4.4% this year.

One final strong point to add is the increase in FCCO's tangible book value over the last year. At the end of 2024, TBV for the company equaled $16.93 per share. As of December 31, 2025, it was $19.84, an improvement of 17.2%. FCCO currently trades at a price to TBV ratio of 1.45.

Last week, I wrote an article for another South Carolina based bank, Southern First Bancshares (SFST). As part of that analysis, I noted that banks in that part of the country have recently earned a takeover premium well above the national average of 1.5x TBV. TowneBank (TOWN) completed a recent acquisition of Dogwood State Bank, which had operations in both North and South Carolina, paying 2.12x TBV, so the current valuation of FCCO still looks attractive at this time.

Additional Risks to Consider

A significant portion of First Community Bank’s outstanding loans are for commercial real estate. Many are forecasting challenges for CRE in the year ahead as loans with variable interest rates reprice to higher levels. Optimism for key metrics like rental rates, leasing activity, and vacancies has declined from 2025. Elevated interest rates, cost of capital, and potential changes in tax policy are all considered potential headwinds for CRE in the year ahead. Given the remarkably low number of non-performing loans on the books for First Community at the end of last year, there is really only one direction in which that number can go.

An added concern is linked to the second largest segment of the company’s loan mix, construction loans. While these loans often generate high levels of interest income, they also come with elevated risks as banks are not in position to finish construction projects that are left incomplete when a construction loan defaults.

First Community would be adversely impacted by a slowdown in economic activity caused by rising unemployment, declines in consumer confidence, or an unexpected rise in inflation caused by trade conflicts or tariffs. Another prolonged government shutdown could constrict growth in a number of businesses in South Carolina that depend on federal contracts. The U.S. government provides almost a third of the state's total budget funding.

After trading in the range of Buy or Strong Buy for most of 2025, First Community Corporation is now considered a Sell by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating. The stock gets below average marks in both Valuation and Profitability. Both scores are impacted by lower cash flow numbers reported by the FCCO over the last 12 months.

Conclusion

A big factor for me when I analyze small cap regional banks like First Community is the service area in which it operates. Even the best run bank can only produce limited opportunities in a slow growth, or negative growth, environment. First Community has access to South Carolina, which has the highest GDP growth on the East Coast. With the Signature Bank addition, the company now has access to a very affluent suburb of Atlanta. Not only does this make the bank more attractive to me as an investor, but it also may put the bank on the radar for larger banks that could be looking for an acquisition of their own. I still believe FCCO is a Buy for the year ahead

data.title: Big Yellow Group: Revenue Growth Is Slowing
data.description:
British self-storage market leader Big Yellow Group (BYLOF) is performing solidly, though I think the growth opportunities once seen in the U.K. self-storage industry may be receding as it becomes more developed.

I last covered the name in my August “sell” piece, Big Yellow: Solid Business But Still Overpriced. As the title suggests, my basic thesis then was that Big Yellow is a proven, sizeable operator in a market with significant ongoing growth potential but that the shares were overpriced.

Since then, the share has moved up another 11%. However, I maintain the same opinion I had then.

Revenue Growth is Slowing Down

A trading statement last week provided a summary of 2025 performance ahead of the final results, which will be published in due course.

Revenue both for the quarter and year inched up 2%.

I see that as a slightly disappointing result. Revenue growth of 2% was slightly behind the 2.2% increase in maximum lettable area, suggesting that occupancy fell and/or the company did not increase pricing.

Closing occupancy did indeed fall, by 0.8% versus the end of 2024. That is not a huge difference, but it is still movement in the wrong direction for a company I continue to see as being in a growth stage. Closing net rent per square foot ended the year 3% higher than at the prior year end. So the problem here is not one of failing to push up prices, but rather of occupancy falling.

In the statement, the company’s chief executive said:

In our interim results, we highlighted the need to return occupancy to being a key driver of revenue growth. We have seen an increase in demand starting from the beginning of November and are pleased to be reporting an improvement in relative occupancy performance over the quarter and have narrowed further the year-on-year like-for-like gap.

That is encouraging, but I think the company has more work to do to reassure investors on this point. This will be a key metric to watch over the coming year.

Expanding the estate is all well and good – four more sites are planned to open in the coming financial year – but maintaining if not growing occupancy levels at existing sites is critical to long-term profitability growth. The year end closing occupancy of 75.4% compares to 78.1% for rival Safestore (SFSHF). Safestore added 4.9% to its revenue last year, handily outperforming Big Yellow in that respect. So Big Yellow’s performance here is not an industry-wide weakness. In fairness, Big Yellow’s performance on rent per square foot was much better than Safestore’s, but given the difference between their portfolio locations, I would not necessarily read too much into that. Sites in cities like London ought to be able to command higher prices than elsewhere.

The picture here overall concerns me a bit, though. For decades the investment case for self-storage firms in the U.K. has been partly about the growth potential in the market, where penetration remains notably lower than the U.S. But Big Yellow is the market leader, and while it continues to grow its estate size, a quarter of its capacity lies unused. That number has been moving slightly higher, not lower, in recent times.

This may suggest that growth is slowing in the sector, and that could be a permanent not temporary phenomenon. Big Yellow’s revenue has been growing annually, and, based on the trading statement, that looks set to continue. But that growth has been slowing, from 10% in 2022 to 6% in 2023 to 2% in 2024 and the same level last year.

The Share Price Still Looks Too High

In my opinion, the share remains overpriced, and accordingly I maintain my “sell” rating.

The current market cap is £2.0bn. Net debt was £0.4bn (as of the end of September), making for an enterprise value of £2.4bn.

The most recently reported six-monthly cash flow from operating activities was £57m. Extrapolating that in a straight line suggests a full-year sum of £114m; the most recent year’s reported full-year figure was £112m, so this seems about right. On that basis, the current enterprise value is around 21 times operating cash flow, which strikes me as expensive. That is the basis of my “sell” rating, on this as a business.

But it is possible to take a contrary view, seeing Big Yellow purely in real estate terms. In its full-year report for the year to the end of September, the company reported a net asset value per share of £13.67. The current share price of £10.02 represents a 27% discount to that valuation, which could support a higher share price.

I value it as a business, not purely as a real estate play, so I maintain my “sell” rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Plains All American Vs. USA Compression: Assessing Sustainability Of 8%+ Yields
data.description:
Intro

Lately, I’ve been searching for quality 8%+ yielders, and honestly, it hasn’t been as easy as it looked at first. You open your favorite screener, set the minimum yield to 8%, maybe add a few extra filters, and it all seems straightforward, right? Plenty of names pop up. But once you start digging deeper, most of them show clear signs that their yields aren’t sustainable over the long term — at least from my perspective, given the fairly strict criteria I use to evaluate them.

Still, as they say, a savvy hunter doesn’t give up easily. After digging through dozens of names, I finally found a few that actually tick the boxes I was looking for. And that search led me to one clear conclusion: for an income‑focused investor looking for sustainable yield, one sector currently stands out from the rest — the midstream segment of the energy infrastructure space. That’s where you find high‑yield names with genuinely sustainable prospects far more often than anywhere else.

For those unfamiliar with midstream companies: put simply, they provide the critical infrastructure that connects the upstream side of the energy ecosystem (oil and gas producers like Exxon and Chevron) with downstream demand — refiners, utilities supplying power as well as the rapidly growing electricity needs of AI data centers, petrochemical plants producing plastics and chemicals, industrial manufacturers such as fertilizer and steel producers, and oil and LNG export terminals. Midstream firms move oil and natural gas through the pipelines and related infrastructure they own, earning fees based on the volumes transported. They also offer additional services such as gas processing, NGL fractionation, terminal logistics, and the increasingly important management of produced water.

As long as oil and gas production remains at reasonable profitability levels, those volumes will continue to flow through midstream systems — and midstream companies will continue to get paid. Their business models are largely insulated from commodity price swings, with most firms having little to no direct exposure to volatile oil and gas prices

Most midstream companies are set up as Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs), which means investors are technically unitholders instead of stockholders, and getting distributions instead of dividends, though buying and selling work just like with any other public equity. You can find more details about the pros and cons of MLPs here.

While MLPs carry certain risks — mainly higher leverage due to their capital‑intensive nature — they are generally well suited for income‑focused investors. Their business model is built on long‑term contracts that provide steady, predictable cash flow, and maximizing distributions is a core part of how they operate.

After recently analyzing Western Midstream (WES), the AMLP (AMLP) ETF (which holds 13 MLPs in the sector), and just last week Hess Midstream (HESM), the attractive high‑yield opportunity in the midstream space pushed me to dig deeper into additional names. This time, I picked two 8%+ yielders at once — both to weigh on each company’s strengths and weaknesses on its own and to compare them side by side to see whether one clearly stands out or whether they might actually complement each other well.

Both companies are also included in the AMLP ETF as well - Plains All American Pipeline (PAA; PAGP) is the fund’s largest holding, making up 13.3% as of writing, while USA Compression Partners (USAC) represents about 4%.

Both Companies Fall Within the Midstream Category, but Operate in Different Segments of the Value Chain

In USAC’s case, I think it’s worth noting from the beginning that it has nothing to do with pipes. Instead, it offers contract compression services, which are crucial for moving natural gas from the wellhead into the gathering system, keeping the pressure needed for the gas to reach its destination.

USAC describes its activities as follows:

We focus on providing midstream natural gas compression services to infrastructure applications primarily in high-volume gathering systems, processing facilities, and transportation applications.

Plains, on the other hand, is a pretty typical midstream MLP play and has the following summary of its activities:

We provide competitive and efficient midstream infrastructure and logistics services to producers, refiners, and other customers. We strive to address regional supply and demand imbalances for crude oil and NGL in the United States and Canada by combining our commercial and operational expertise with the strategic location and capabilities of our transportation, terminalling, storage, processing, and fractionation assets.

In short, while both are defined as midstreams, their operating segments are actually quite different.

Companies in Brief

Plains All American

Plains All American dates back to 1981, when its predecessor Plains Resources began as a small oil and gas exploration and production company. Plains’ growth accelerated in the early 1990s, highlighted by the construction of the Cushing Terminal in 1993–1994 — then the largest infrastructure project west of the Mississippi, with 2 million barrels of storage. Over time, the company expanded the site to 27 million barrels.

In 1998, Plains acquired the All American Pipeline and completed the IPO of Plains All American Pipeline L.P., bringing its common units to the public market. After decades of strategic M&A, the company continued its bolt‑on expansion between 2022 and 2025, investing roughly $3.7 billion across 16 acquisitions to further extend and strengthen its business.

Today, Plains All American is one of the largest crude oil-focused midstream companies in North America, and it is also the dominant operator in the Permian Basin, where it controls one of the largest crude gathering and transportation networks in the region.

The EIA map does a great job of highlighting the largest basins in the U.S. The Permian, where PAA holds a leading position, is considered the world’s largest oil basin, boasting vast resources and some of the most economical drilling opportunities in the U.S.

The Bakken basin has the highest breakeven prices for producing oil, while the Permian basins has the lowest:

Plains has a unique ownership structure and tax arrangement, with PAA operating as a typical MLP and PAGP serving as its publicly traded, K-1-free counterpart, while also being PAA’s parent. This setup gives investors the flexibility to choose the tax scheme that works best for them while investing in the same business. Investing in PAGP right now could be a smart choice, as the company isn’t expected to pay corporate income taxes until 2032 thanks to a $1.2 billion deferred tax asset it holds and is depreciating.

Plains’ operating profile in numbers:

..and the operating footprint map:

USAC

USA Compression Partners was founded in 1998 with a focused strategy: providing large‑horsepower, standardized compression units for midstream infrastructure. Backed by private capital, the company grew rapidly, surpassing 600,000 horsepower by 2010. USAC went public in 2013 and expanded its capabilities through the acquisition of S&R Compression.

In 2018, a $1.7 billion transaction with Energy Transfer significantly broadened USAC’s geographic reach, with Energy Transfer becoming the controlling owner. Today, USAC operates 3.9 million horsepower across nearly all major U.S. shale basins, making it one of the largest independent natural gas compression providers alongside Archrock (AROC) and Kodiak Gas Services (KGS). The company continues to follow its original model of focusing on large‑horsepower infrastructure applications.

Latest financial results and outlooks for USAC and Plains

Plains

Plains’ latest Q3 GAAP EPS came in at $0.44, beating estimates by $0.07. Revenue was $11.58 billion, down 7.1% year‑over‑year and missing expectations by about $820 million.

Key highlights from the third quarter of 2025:

YoY adjusted EBITDA dynamics:

.. and the outlook for the rest of the year, including the EPIC acquisition:

USAC

Q3 GAAP EPS was $0.26, beating estimates by $0.04. Revenue came in at $250.3 million, up 4.3% year‑over‑year and roughly in line with expectations.

Q3 operating highlights, as presented:

Acquisitions

Both companies have recently completed major acquisitions. For Plains, along with making a significant purchase, the company also sold its large NGL business.

Plains

The acquisition of EPIC wasn’t about buying an entirely new company — they simply purchased the remaining 45% stake they didn’t previously own.

Plains sold its NGL business to help finance the purchase and better align with management’s strategic vision. Once the deals are closed, Plains will become a more oil-focused, pure-play company.

In addition, as a result of these transactions, the company also reduces its commodity-related income exposure:

USAC

USA Compression Partners is acquiring J‑W Power Co. for about $860 million, a move that is said to expand its compression fleet, broaden its geographic footprint, and add diversified revenue streams such as aftermarket services, parts distribution, and specialized manufacturing.

USAC expects the deal to be accretive to distributable cash flow and values the acquisition at roughly 5.8× estimated 2026 adjusted EBITDA, with the transaction helping accelerate its path to leverage below 4.0×. The consideration includes $430 million in cash and 18.3 million newly issued units, with the cash portion funded through its revolving credit facility. Closing is expected in early 2026 pending regulatory approval.

The newly merged company could potentially enjoy strong synergies thanks to its expanded geographical reach.

Valuation

I believe the best way to value midstream companies is by using P/FCF or EV/EBITDA. It’s also important to look at their leverage profile and distribution‑coverage metrics. From what I gather, midstreams generally aren’t designed to run with low leverage. Instead, they usually operate with as much leverage as their financials and creditworthiness can support, keeping high distribution yields as a central part of their strategy.

I think it provides a great overview of companies to display the Plains and USAC metrics side by side in a table for better comparison.

PAA/PAGP (Plains All American Pipeline)

USAC (USA Compression Partners)

Notes

Credit Rating

BBB / BBB / Baa2 (Investment Grade) ~5%

Ba3/B+/BB (Non‑IG / High Yield)

PAA has a lower credit risk

Leverage Ratio (Net Debt/EBITDA)

~3.3, projected to stay in the range 3.25-3.75

3.89 as of Q3/25

USAC operates with higher leverage

Cash Coverage of Distribution

projected fwd at 1.75, but recent quarters have shown negative cash flow after distributions

1.61 as of Q3/25. There have been recent quarters reported where cash flow didn’t fully cover distributions

Both companies face their own coverage challenges, and recent M&A activity is adding noise to the numbers. Both companies have a strong outlook for covering the distribution with cash.

Forward EV/EBITDA

8.1, 50% preferred equity included as debt

9.3

USAC trades at a premium

Income Diversification

55% from Permian, oil concentrated, the other 3 basins each contribute evenly, 15%

60% of income comes from Permian and the Gulf Coast

Both are equally well diversified regionally

Author's Creation.

To sum up, the comparison table shows that both companies operate with high leverage, with USAC slightly higher on a trailing basis prior to the latest J-W Power acquisition, though still within a typical range for midstreams. It’s worth highlighting that USAC’s non‑investment‑grade credit ratings lag behind those of Plains, which remain in investment grade.

Both companies have recently reported a few quarters where free cash flow didn’t fully cover distributions, but I think this falls within normal volatility, and coverage is expected to improve moving forward.

It’s worth noting that recent acquisitions can make trailing metrics less relevant, as future performance will hinge on how well these assets are integrated and how closely actual outcomes match pre-acquisition expectations.

In my view, both companies have a well-balanced regional footprint and, with most of their business focused in the Permian Basin, which is often considered the most valuable basin in the U.S.

If we look at both companies individually from a valuation perspective, Plains seems to be slightly undervalued as its peers typically trade in the EV/EBITDA range of 9 to 13, while Plains is currently at 8.1.

USAC seems fairly valued compared to its closest peers, AROC and KGS, both of which trade around an EV/EBITDA ratio of 9.

I think for Plains and USAC, from a valuation perspective, it's sufficient to focus on EV/EBITDA multiple comparisons this time, since their P/FCF metrics are highly volatile due to recent M&A activity.

I rate both Plains and USAC as a Buy. Plains appear slightly undervalued compared to peers, and its recent M&A activity offers better visibility into its prospects, positioning it as an oil pure play in the future. The distribution yield for PAA or the dividend yield for PAGP are also very attractive at current levels.

For USAC, the positive rating also mainly comes from its compelling dividend yield backed by solid financials, along with the potential synergies from the recent acquisition of J-W Power, which I believe was purchased at an great value.

Risks

In my view, the most important risk for both companies is the oil price. While neither company has direct exposure to commodity prices fluctuations, a meaningful drop in oil would likely pressure drilling activity, which in turn reduces the oil and gas volumes that need to move from upstream to downstream. As long as WTI (CL1:COM) stays above roughly $55, it’s not a big concern in my view, but a sustained drop below that level, while not an immediate issue, could eventually start to impact their businesses. On the other hand, strong AI‑driven energy demand and the resulting support for natural gas prices help offset some of these risks.

In addition, J.P. Morgan analyst warns of geopolitical risks in light of the latest developments in Venezuela:

the evolving situation in Venezuela could immediately represent one of the largest upside risks to the global oil supply outlook for 2026–2027 and beyond.

Takeaway

When comparing Plains and USAC, I think it makes great sense to invest in both at the same time. Their leverage levels are broadly similar, but they operate in different parts of the value chain, which in my opinion reduces risk through diversification. That said, both are highly leveraged, though still within a range that isn’t considered alarming for midstream. Even so, it’s wise to monitor their quarterly reports closely, especially leverage trends and how well cash flow covers distributions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SRJN: A 6.375% Junior Subordinated Notes IPO From Spire
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

This is our next article, we turn our attention to a recently listed debt issue on the exchange: Spire Inc.'s (SR) 6.375% Junior Subordinated Notes (SRJN) due 2086. As usual in this type of analysis, we'll run the new debt issue through our fixed-income IPO evaluation routine to determine if it can be a valuable addition to our portfolio. The examination will begin with a brief study of the issuing company to assess the extent to which we can feel safe holding its subordinated notes. After that, we'll conduct a relative evaluation of SRJN, comparing its metrics to peers with similar metrics.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information about the newly listed baby bond SRJN. Here, we will highlight only the most important metrics for the analysis that follows.

SRJN pays 6.375% annual interest in quarterly distributions of $0.3984 each. Interest on the junior subordinated notes accrues from January 12, 2026, starting distributions on June 1, 2026. This means the first interest payment will be irregular in size, amounting to approximately $0.6154. The company has the option, at its discretion, to defer interest payments on the notes for up to 40 consecutive quarters, on more than one occasion. The company's gross proceeds from the new security are $200 million for a total of 8 million junior subordinated notes issued with a liquidation value of $25 each. The new baby bond was rated BBB- by S&P at its IPO, and the junior subordinated debt of Spire is rated Baa3 by Moody's. SRJN is a long-term junior debt obligation of Spire with a maturity date of 03/01/2086 and is callable on and after 03/01/2031.

As the issue is just listed on the exchange, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, SRJN is priced at $24.92 with a Yield to Maturity calculated by the XIRR function of 6.56%. The tables below show the beginning of the internal rate of return calculation and how the YTM of the security would change with a price variation around the current price:

In a chart visualization:

Using the same internal rate of return method, the Yield to Call is calculated to be 6.66%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

Company basics

Founded in 1857 and headquartered in Saint Louis, Missouri, Spire Inc. is a publicly traded regulated natural gas utility company serving Alabama, Mississippi, and Missouri.

It operates approximately 63,000 miles of pipelines, providing utility services to 1.7 million homes and businesses in these states.

The quite recent "big thing" about the company was the announced in July 2025 acquisition of Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee business from Duke Energy Corporation (DUK) and the associated expansion to a new state.

The acquisition of the $2.48 billion enterprise value on a cash-free, debt-free basis, subject to customary closing adjustments, is on track to close in the First Quarter of 2026. It will increase Spire's pipeline mileage by over 7,000 and the customers by more than 200,000.

Company Performance

This is how the general market has evaluated the common stock of the company, SR, for the past 10 years:

We'll compare the total return performance for the past ten years of Spire Inc. to that of the broader market, iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), iShares U.S. Utilities ETF (IDU) as a representative for the US utilities, and to the Tortoise North American Pipeline Index ETF (TPYP), as a specific representative for the average North American gas pipeline company:

The comparison shows that Spire is lagging behind all these ETFs. While SR has managed only recently to surpass its pre-COVID total return values, the ETFs we observe have done so in a much faster manner, nowadays standing at two or more times the 10-year total return of Spire.

The expected future performance of Spire Inc. is more on the cautious side, according to the majority of the SA Analysts and the Quant rating, giving the company the neutral "Hold" score. The Wall Street analysts are more optimistic regarding the SR's future performance, with an average "Buy" rating.

The direct comparison to Gas Utilities shows that while Spire underperforms the National Fuel Gas Company (NFG) and the big dog in the sector - Atmos Energy Corporation (ATO), it performs in line with the average US-regulated Gas Utility:

Capital Structure

Below is Spire's capital structure as of the Third Quarter of 2025. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also presented for comparison in the short term.

As of the end of September 2025, the total debt outstanding of Spire was $5.24 billion. SRJN will increase this value by $200 million to a total of $5.44 billion. The company has $242 million in preferred equity, junior to the debt, and at the lowest position in the capital structure is the common equity of approximately $3.15 billion. We can calculate a Debt-to-Equity ratio of 1.6, above the average of 1.36 for the regulated US gas utilities, as of January 2026.

Dividends

SR has an impressive dividend history of 36 consecutive years of uninterrupted distributions, the last 22 of them of dividend growth.

SR's TTM dividend amounts to $3.18, and with the last closing common share price of $84.06, the stock's distribution yield is 3.78%. This is what the dividend yield has looked like for the past 10 years:

The forward estimations for SR's distribution and dividend yield look like this:

Spire Inc. has approximately 59 million common shares outstanding. With a projected forward annual distribution of $3.30 on each, we can calculate $194.7 million in annual dividends for common shares. The company had annual TTM total interest expenses of approximately $204 million, and the new issue will increase this number by $12.8 million to approximately $221 million. On an annual basis, the common shares' distribution is enough to cover 88% of the expected total interest expenses.

Credit Rating

This is the title of the rating action with which S&P affirms the 'BBB+' credit rating of Spire inc., but also decreases the Outlook for it to "Negative", with respect to the aforementioned acquisition of Piedmont Natural Gas Tennessee business from Duke Energy Corporation. Here are S&P's reasons that triggered the Outlook revision:

The negative outlooks on Spire, Spire Missouri, and Spire Alabama reflect the possibility that consolidated FFO to debt could weaken to consistently below our 12% downgrade threshold. Our base case assumes the company’s FFO to debt weakens to the 11%-12% range following Spire’s acquisition of the Tennessee local distribution assets.

We could lower our ratings on Spire, Spire Missouri, and Spire Alabama over the next 18 months if Spire maintains consolidated FFO to debt of below 12%. This could occur if anticipated growth from the acquisition does not materialize, or the company funds the acquisition with a greater-than-anticipated level of debt.

Spire's OTC bonds

The image above shows how Spire's senior debt issues are currently priced. As SRJN is a long-term obligation of the company, we'll compare its yield to the 5.67% YTW of the longest-term OTC senior bond (of a subsidiary) - the '51 issue, rated A1 by Moody's. Above, we calculated a YTM of 6.56% for SRJN. Being five notches lower in credit quality than the '51 senior note, I'd say the 90 basis points higher yield for SRJN makes it overvalued to the senior debt of comparable duration.

Here, we can make another consideration, too. The '51 senior debt issue is priced with approximately 85 basis points above the US treasuries with the longest duration:

This credit spread is above the average of 60 basis points for the US corporate-issued Single-A debt issue:

Investors are requiring a higher credit spread for the senior debt of Spire, compared with the average peers of the same credit quality, indicating a cautious approach. Not only is SRJN overvalued to the senior debt of the company, but the senior debt itself is indicating a weakness. Potential investors should consider this when making a decision.

Peers comparison

The Gas Utilities sector does not have other listed debt issues, so I'll use all the priced below par term Utilities baby bonds for the peers comparison.

The securities with their metrics:

The comparison shows that SRJN is priced very close to two other Utilities baby bond additions, which IPOs we have recently covered - DTK and XELLL. I personally am more interested in the discounted Utilities baby bonds, which offer similar YTM, but better appreciation potential on a downshift in the long-term treasury yields. It is worth mentioning Entergy's baby bonds ELC, EMP, and EAI, offering considerably better credit quality at 40-50 basis points lower YTM.

Use of Proceeds

We estimate that we will receive net proceeds of approximately $193.5 million from the sale of the Notes in this offering after deducting the underwriting discount and estimated offering expenses payable by us. We estimate that we will receive net proceeds of approximately $222.5 million if the underwriters exercise in full their over-allotment option to purchase up to an additional $30.0 million in principal amount of the Notes. We intend to use such net proceeds, along with other funds, to redeem all of the outstanding shares of our Series A Preferred Stock with an aggregate $250.0 million liquidation preference, upon which the corresponding 10,000,000 outstanding depositary shares representing our Series A Preferred Stock will also be redeemed, and for other general corporate purposes. For the avoidance of doubt, nothing contained in this prospectus supplement constitutes a notice of redemption of our Series A Preferred Stock or such depositary shares.

Source: SRJN Prospectus

Now, this is an interesting moment. Spire is issuing a 6.375% junior subordinated debt to redeem a lower-positioned security in the capital structure, with a lower coupon.

Apparently, the reason for this is that the preferred stock dividends are not tax-deductible, unlike the interest on the junior subordinated notes, which will reduce the issuer's taxable income. Since this was also true when SR-A was issued, one has to wonder why the preferred stock was issued in the first place.

Optional Redemptions

We may redeem the Notes at our option before their maturity: (i) in whole or in part, on one or more occasions at any time on or after March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 100% of the principal amount of the Notes being redeemed plus accrued and unpaid interest; (ii) in whole, but not in part, at any time before March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 100% of their principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest thereon, if certain changes in tax laws, regulations or interpretations occur; or (iii) in whole, but not in part, at any time before March 1, 2031, at a redemption price equal to 102% of their principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest thereon, if a rating agency makes certain changes in the equity credit criteria for securities such as the Notes.

Source: SRJN Prospectus

Addition to the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF

The face value outstanding of the new exchange-traded baby bond SRJN of $200 million makes it a potential addition to the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index during some of its upcoming rebalancing. If that happens, it will also be included in the holdings of the main benchmark iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). PFF is an exchange-traded fund that seeks to track the investment results of the above-mentioned index and is rather important for us due to its influence on the behavior of many of the exchange-traded securities.

Conclusion

SRJN is a borderline investment-grade baby bond. The recently downgraded outlook of Spire's credit score heed for caution when making investment decisions about the company's debt. A downgrade of the company's credit score will drive its junior subordinated debt into the high-yield territory. At this point, SRJN looks overvalued to Spire's senior long-term debt, and unappealing compared to Utilities' baby bond peers.

data.title: Dycom Industries (And Me) To The AI/Data-Centers Bubble Bears: Just Saying 'No!'
data.description:
The 1980s and ‘90s called—They want their wires, coaxial cables, and fibre optics back.

Z-z-z-z.

That’s how I reacted when I first looked at a business description for Dycom Industries, Inc. (DY).

But upon further review, I see good things.

The stock rates quite well in Seeking Alpha. Its Quant Rating is Strong Buy. I like that. And as we’ll see below, this rating is very well deserved. The Wall Street rating is also a Strong Buy. The SA Analysts Rating is Buy.

Beyond that, I like shares of companies with boring businesses.

So, too, does legendary former Fidelity Magellan Fund (FMAGX) manager Peter Lynch. Chapter 8 of his classic One Up on Wall Street was titled “The Perfect Stock, What a Deal.”

He discussed 13 perfect stock traits.

Number one consists of companies having dull or ridiculous names. DY doesn’t count here. But it does fit the runner-up trait, “It Does Something Dull”

The idea is to invest at prices that haven’t been elevated by favorable publicity or sentiment. We can wait till later on, when prices are boosted by earnings.

My appreciation for boring businesses led me to a bunch of stocks in the Engineering and Construction Industry.

So much the better if boring businesses are also very profitable and growing briskly. That works for Engineering and Construction…

It’s easy to see our infrastructure is aging. So, there’s more and more business coming to companies working to upgrade and/or maintain it.

But I hadn’t thought of DY’s bailiwick, “the planning and design of aerial, underground, and buried fiber optic, copper, and coaxial cable systems,” etc. (quote from the Seeking Alpha Company Profile) as a standout within the industry.

Such activities proceeded briskly in the latter part of the 20th century. Companies worked hard to roll out cable TV, broadband internet, and mobile communications. They spoke of building an “information superhighway.”

Congratulations to the industry!

It succeeded. The superhighway exists. We routinely burn rubber today as we speed along this highway.

So why get excited now, past the quarter mark of the 21st century? It was a great story. But is it still fresh current?

There are two affirmative answers. Both contribute to bullish investment cases for DY:

  1. The traditional wiring business still has far more life than many realize, and
  2. A recent acquisition makes DV a serious AI-Data-center play.

So let’s call DY boring-and-then some.

Wiring in 2026 and Beyond

At a 12/9/25 conference, CEO Daniel Peyovich introduced the company in a way that matched my basic assumption.

We're across all 50 states. We do both wireline and wireless infrastructure for the major telecommunications and cable customers. We're across all 50 states. We do both wireline and wireless infrastructure for the major telecommunications and cable customers…

Yes indeed, an obvious 20th-century business.

Or not…

Peyovich may have been anticipating my sensibilities when he continued thusly:

I think that we often hear that people believe because it's been going on for some time now that it's already reached some kind of pinnacle or it's already starting to decelerate when, in fact, the opposite is true. Most of the customers that we have today are continuing to ramp their programs. There's obviously a lot of bullishness around getting fiber deployed.

The number, if you take the passings collectively across our customers, if you look at the total homes in America, it gets to about 80% roughly or about 120 million, 125 million passings. So there's still a ton of work to do, and that's going to continue to ramp in the coming years….

[T]he passings, the number of passings per year are going to continue to grow over the next several years. You certainly have -AT&T has been very vocal about their continued growth. You have many other customers that fall into that as well.

But DY charges by the foot, not by the home. And the spaces with which it deals are getting more expansive.

Lower-hanging fruit (e.g., 40-foot lot lines) was built out long ago. Today, DY is dealing with more expansive lot lines, especially as it wires more rural areas. And the company today is dealing with more (expensive) underground installations.

Underscoring the importance of getting everybody everywhere wired (thinking of broadband as an essential utility), we have a U.S. program known as BEAD (Broadband Equity and Access Deployment). It’s administered by the NTIA (National Telecommunications and Information Administration).

This could eventually bring $26 billion in new revenue to DY for fiber or HFC (hybrid fiber coaxial) cable. None of this has yet hit DY’s revenues or backlog. But these multiyear outlays are likely to start kicking in during the fiscal year starting 2/1/26. DY has already received a $500 million verbal award.

And by the way, it’s not only about new installations. We need a lot of work on the existing infrastructure. As Peyovich said at the 12/9/25 conference:

You have infrastructure that's been there for 25, 30 years. The capacity is even close to what they're looking for today. And very importantly, the latency is not even in the ballpark. And they also need redundancy. So if something does get hit in the field, you don't want to take down their entire cloud, their entire network. So they are looking for redundancy as well.

It sounds here like Peyovich is talking about bigger things than home installations. That’s so. He’s referring to…

connecting ultimately data center to data center across the country, you're talking about tens of thousands of miles.

So, I was wrong. The wiring business is much bigger—and more contemporary and stretching at least into the 2030s—than I realized.

All in on Data-centers

Did you notice, in the last quote, the reference to connecting data centers? It’s been top of mind for DY.

Connecting data centers is all well and good. We know how important these are.

But there’s more to be done than just connecting them. How about getting inside the fence—moving from the public right of way to inside the actual data center campus.

That’s exactly what DY did when, on 12/23/25, it completed its acquisition of Power Solutions.

The latter is an electrical contractor that focuses on doing work in data centers. And within this sphere, Power does a lot.

This, according to DY, amounts to a compelling adjacency.

Take a look at the revenue numbers at the bottom of the above image. Financially speaking, Power Solutions is transformative for DY.

The purchase is expensive:

The purchase price on a cash-free, debt-free basis was comprised of approximately $1.63 billion of cash, including an estimated working capital adjustment, and approximately 1.0 million shares of Dycom common stock.

But DY expects the acquisition to immediately be accretive to earnings.

More importantly, it makes DY a major player in data-center construction.

Schematically and superficially, though, one has cause to wonder..

DY has a nationwide presence. Power Solutions, on the other hand, does most of its work in a single region.

Geographically, Power Solutions looks measly, almost like a pimple on an elephant.

But if Power Solutions is to be mainly in one region, oh what region that is!

It’s known as DMV (D.C, Maryland, Virginia). Some refer to part of the territory as “data center alley.” That’s because it accounts for more than 35% of overall U.S. datacenter capacity.

Juxtaposing the single Power Solutions region with the nationwide DY presence suggests a big opportunity for DY to cross-sell data-center electrical contracting.

That’s exactly how DY sees it. Note the title of the above slide. Note too the cross-selling aspirations expressed below.

This appears to be a great move for DY!

But it does force us to squarely confront a serious and controversial issue…

Talk of an AI/Data Center Bubble

It’s no secret that a lot of folks harken back to the turn-of-the-century telecom bubble and predict a repeat. Given the way DY just went very much all in on data center construction, anybody who is bullish on this stock has to definitively reject the bubble scenario.

I am among those who see bubble talk as misguided.

It’s different this time—Yes, I said it. Many love to attack those they catch saying things like this.

History means something, the bears insist. Maybe it repeats. Maybe it rhymes. Either way, ignore it at your peril.

I agree!

History, or as I prefer to say, precedent, is important. But that’s so only if, if, if you analyze it correctly.

What exactly is it we should expect to repeat or rhyme?

Bears point to the turn-of-the-century grotesque telecom overbuilding and the horrifying correction that ensued and destroyed much shareholder wealth, including, famously, the bad things that happened to those who owned shares of big suppliers like Cisco (CSCO).

But that is the wrong precedent (history) to consider.

Lawyers, who intensely study and live under stare decisis, the doctrine of precedent, will tell you precedent is meaningful if, and only if, the key facts are similar.

That is not even remotely so in the case of CSCO and circa-2000 telecom.

I, who was already a veteran analyst back then and had a front-row seat to all of it, discussed this in considerable detail in my 1/24/26 report on Sterling Infrastructure (STRL).

It would be a lot to repeat here in its entirety. So I strongly, very strongly, urge you to read that write-up if you have any interest in DY.

Briefly, very briefly, the telecom companies that failed back then were, pardon me for speaking bluntly, commercially useless. They had to fail. Their business models were dumb and resulted entirely from political activism. There was no consumer or enterprise demand.

Nobody today who has used AI in a grown-up way sees it along those lines. Real AI users laugh at the garbage (my opinion) coming out of Magnificent 7 conference rooms and know that stuff doesn’t deserve to ever make money.

So we couldn’t care less about how observers today complain nobody is profiting from AI. (Actually, though, Palantir (PLTR) is profiting handsomely.)

Legit AI users understand what really works and are already seeing serious benefits emerging out of the grassroots, from pioneers and little companies you haven’t yet heard of, just like at one time, nobody ever heard of Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), or Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Google.

The real (repeatable, relevant) precedent is the way tech as a whole (computers, mobile, Internet, etc.) has evolved with new previously-unknown winners and far, far more value creation than even the early bulls dared imagine.

DY’s Numbers

These are going to look pretty good. (Remember, the Seeking Alpha Quant Rating is Strong Buy.)

Bear in mind, though, that most of these numbers do not reflect Power Solutions. The deal closed after the latest period included in the historic numbers.

But given the likely impact of Power Solutions, consider many of these numbers as bases upon which DY is likely to build.

Let’s start with quarterly revenue and EPS trends.

There’s no cause for complaint here!

Let's now move on to the historic and forward growth rates.

(I compare DY to medians among companies in the Construction and Engineering industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

How about those historical growth rates!

They look about even with the already-strong industry medians. But remember, they don’t include Power Solutions. They reflect only the businesses I erroneously assumed were 1980s–’90s relics.

The core fundamentals also reflect only pre-Power Solutions business.

Here, too, DY gives a good account of itself,

Finally, here are the Financial Statement Highlights.

The table looks fine.

But as we consider the future, we have to be realistic. The Power Solutions purchase will add a lot of new revenue, gross profits, and operating profits. But it will also add a lot of new debt and equity.

The main thing to consider here is whether the company’s history suggests DY appears to have been good enough at financial management to justify an assumption it will be able to handle its large new acquisition. I believe the answer is “yes.”

Risk

Obviously, when a company makes a huge acquisition, we have to be alert to possible integration issues and operating challenges.

Here, at least, DY management seems to be very much wired into (pun completely intended) the need to absorb lots of new people.

Indeed, Peyovich said on the 11/19/25 earnings call:

With the addition of Power Solutions talented team of over 2,800 to our current team of over 16,100, we will have a combined highly skilled workforce of 19,000 people. This is a massive competitive differentiator that enables us to meet the growing needs across our collective customers.

It seems DY went into this as ready as any acquirer has ever been to integrate a large new workforce.

And, of course, there’s the risky elephant in the room — whether or not an AI/data-center bubble is poised to burst.

I’ve explained above and here why I do not believe this to be so. But everyone is entitled to their opinion. If you disagree with mine, DY is not a stock for you.

What to Do About DY Stock

Here are DY’s valuation metrics.

On a PEG basis, DY looks like a bargain compared to the SPY group—higher growth, lower PEG. Of course you have to believe in the growth projection. I do, especially with the addition of Power Solutions.

The price chart is intriguing—and jarring.

Let’s start with the basics. The rising 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) is above the also-rising 50-day EMA. Neither looks like a moonshot. But both are OK. So from a momentum standpoint, DY looks OK, albeit not spectacular.

Now let’s examine Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trade is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Yikes!

Look how deeply negative these indicators have recently been.

Sellers being more motivated than buyers acts as a drag on stock prices. And when we’re seeing so much negativity in CMF, we have to pause and consider why. Marc Chaikin, creator of CMF, describes it as a measure of “smart money” behavior.

It’s not a perfect signal. I’ve seen as much in past backtests I’ve done on it. But it still is an important clue. If we’re to trade against the smart money, it behooves us to at least know why.

A lot, I believe for DY, involves institutional nervousness about the large Power Solutions purchase.

I’ve long observed investor discomfort with big acquisitions. It’s almost as if institutional investor job applicants are asked, on an entrance test, “Do you fear big acquisitions?” If they answer “No,” the application is automatically rejected.

And here, for added urgency, DY’s big acquisition flies directly in the faces of the bubble bears.

So I get it.

I’m usually leery of going against powerful, smart money sentiment. But as noted, they aren’t perfect.

I’ve given this matter careful consideration. And I believe that here, we have an opportunity to buy before the smart money recognizes how well-positioned DY really is for where the world is going. (It’s analogous to the Peter Lynch dull-company investment case—Buy before “they” figure things out and rush in.)

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding, or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew "Strong …" extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think Buy, Hold, or Sell are enough.

I see DY as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating DY as a Buy.

data.title: Uncertainty May Lead To A Longer Fed Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Federal Reserve Open Market Committee voted to keep the fed funds rate the same at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent. The move snaps a three-consecutive-meeting streak where the committee had lowered interest rates by ¼ percent in each meeting. Two members dissented with the committee, each wanting a ¼ percentage point reduction. I believe that future rate changes by the Fed are not a foregone conclusion, and we may discover later this year that we have reached the neutral interest rate.

What The Market Predicts: Fed Fund Futures

The Fed fund futures market is a market where traders can place their bets as to where the Fed funds rate will be in the future. It helps give investors an idea as to where the market expects the Fed funds rate to be in the future. Since the last meeting in December, the fed fund futures for January barely moved, staying near the current midpoint of 3.63%.

In translation, nobody was surprised to see the Fed resume holding interest rates.
