Additionally, the dissent of Trump appointees wanting lower rates is no surprise either, as the Administration continues to call for lower interest rates. Looking ahead, the committee forecasted one more rate cut in 2026, with an average consensus interest rate of 3.4%.

A cut by the Federal Reserve would, in theory, lower the Fed funds rate to 3.33%, or between 3-1/4 and 3-1/2 percent. The Fed fund futures market is currently projecting that interest rate cut to occur in September with the fed funds rate sitting at exactly that level. I believe with the inflation rate above 2% and falling (but not too fast) combined with a slow jobs market (but still creating jobs), the Fed is in no hurry to conduct additional rate cuts.

Looking Ahead: The New Chair

It’s obvious at this point that there will be a new Federal Reserve chairman in May, when Jerome Powell’s term expires. This leaves him with two more meetings as chairman before he is replaced. With a majority of the committee being less dovish than the Trump appointees, I would not be surprised if rates are being held to try to offset the potential of multiple rate cuts with a new chairman and/or additional appointees.

I think the idea of rates holding until at least Powell’s exit is a done deal unless job growth goes negative and/or the inflation rate dives and threatens to push towards zero versus the long-term policy rate of 2%. While job creation has been low, the committee noted that “the unemployment rate has shown some signs of stabilization.” The labor market was the singular reason that the committee began cutting interest rates last fall.

Government Shutdown? Here We Go Again

While it was not on the table a week ago, a new government shutdown appears to be inevitable. Senate Democrats have stated they are not interested in supporting a new appropriations bill that funds the government from February through September. The result is likely to be another government shutdown.

While I have not gotten an explicit definition of whether or not data gathering will be affected by the shutdown, I doubt that the BLS or BEA got the same priority of SNAP benefits when a bipartisan deal was reached several weeks ago. The likely shutdown will once again impact economic data and could come as soon as next Friday’s scheduled January jobs report.

Conclusion

If a government shutdown occurs, it could further incentivize a data-dependent Federal Reserve to keep interest rates the same. The March meeting, which is next, will include a series of forecasted updates for where the Fed sees inflation, unemployment, and the Fed funds rate. Investors will need to look for changes, but based on the likelihood of a government shutdown, I believe data delays and confusion lie ahead.

data.title: Sabre: Shares Implode While Some Green Shoots Emerge
data.description:
Shares of Sabre Corporation (SABR) have seen a dreadful performance as shares of the travel technology business have fallen to lows of just $1 and change. This marks a dreadful performance from an IPO price at roughly 10 times that amount, in an offering that took place more than ten years ago.

This situation has been the outcome of a period of underperforming business performance and a toxic combination of too much debt. Improvements in the core business and a substantial divestment and reasonable prices create some air and time, as investors are no believers in a turnaround, even as some minor green shoots are appearing here.

An Issue Of Debt & Stagnation

Sabre Corp provides trust to the travel ecosystem, being the center of an ecosystem that connects travel suppliers, travel sellers, IT solutions, distribution, and travel buyers.

The business generated about $3.0 billion in sales in 2024 from providing such solutions, and while this number was up minimally, adjusted EBITDA margins improved more than 5 points to 17% of sales. Instrumental in this is a $2.7 billion travel solutions business, with the distribution-part of this involving some 363 million bookings, on which average booking fees approached $6 per share, while the IT solutions served nearly 700 million passengers that year.

The second, and much smaller, business was a $327 million hospitality solutions business, barely making up over 10% of sales, with this business, among others, handling 128 million central reservation system transactions.

While such numbers look impressive, the issue is that this was already a $3 billion business a decade ago, with revenues peaking at $4 billion pre-pandemic, as the company never recovered. While nominal sales are similar to a decade ago, the issue is that of real revenue declines, factoring in the impact of inflation. This was seen with GAAP operating profit margins falling from the higher-teens to the lower-teens. Worse, about 40% dilution has been incurred over this period of time.

The real issue, which created a drag on the business, was that of leverage, a substantial amount of this, and expensive interest rates, leaving no realistic earnings. This made shares essentially a melting ice cube as the company never delivered on its promises.

2024 As A Base

In February of last year, Sabre reported a 4% increase in 2024 sales to $3.03 billion, with adjusted EBITDA up 53% to $517 million. That says very little, as the company incurred an interest bill of around half a billion with adjusted losses reported at $72 million and GAAP losses reported at $278 million, dreadful numbers, even as both improved meaningfully.

Some 385 million shares outstanding traded at just $2 per share, for a market value of just about $800 million, minimal in relation to EBITDA. The issue was that of a $4.3 billion net debt load and over $5.0 billion gross debt load, with effective costs of debt being very high, around 10%, or just above that.

The company aimed to address this as it announced a $1.1 billion deal to sell the hospitality business to TPG Capital in April, with net cash proceeds seen at $960 million, sufficient to reduce net debt by about 20%. With revenues reported at $328 million in 2024, segment margins are seen at just over 10% of sales, as multiples fetched look very reasonable at just over 3 times sales. Activities were valued at a high-twenty times segment EBITDA multiple, as the news sent shares up to $2.50 per share.

Hereafter, reality kicked in with first quarter sales down 1%, the company issuing new bonds over the summer at coupons of 11% and change. Second quarter sales fell another 1%, but third quarter revenue growth returned to positive 3%. Through the first nine months of the year, revenues are flat at $2.1 billion, which is for the remaining travel solutions business, with EBITDA margins for the period up another 2%. Adjusted net losses for the period narrowed from $46 million to $10 million. While this looks encouraging, it, among others, excludes a $35 million stock-based compensation expense.

Year to date, interest expenses of $332 million were basically at par compared to 2024, but this likely will come down with net debt down to $3.7 billion, still highly demanding with adjusted EBITDA seen around $530 million, for a 7 times leverage ratio. This is recognized by the credit marks, with new bonds still carrying 11% and change in terms of the coupons, as issued in November.

What Now?

Trading at just $1 and change, the enterprise valuation of Sabre stands at just over 1 times sales, at about 8 times EBITDA, with no realistic earnings to show for it. Of course, the company did announce (and closed) on a solid sale last year, with the hospitality business sold at around 3 times sales, while its margins were lagging. Continued improvement in the margins and reduced interest expenses make it realistic that losses are rapidly approaching break-even levels and might even turn positive, which is actually encouraging.

This comes as investors are skeptical about this turnaround, and perhaps more skeptical about the competitive positioning of the solutions provided by Sabre. This comes after the business posts flattish sales in a booming air travel market, with AI, and other software developments, of course, hurting prospects for what essentially is a legacy booking/IT/software ecosystem.

The set-up is interesting, with the business performance, notably the margin improvement demonstrated upon, net debt coming down, and expectations only falling. That makes it so that the situation is improving, which is a long way from saying that appeal is seen. For that, the hole for the company to dig out of is huge, as real questions can be asked about the strength of the business model. The model of being a middleman in a world that is getting more transparent is a key risk, alongside other technologies developing, as topline sales growth is lagging the market and peers, as evidence of that.

This makes me very cautious to get involved just yet, but it is the setup for improving bottom-line performance and a lower share price, which makes me inclined to keep an update on the firm into 2026.
